articles.summary:
Google’s historical approach to user data privacy has faced significant scrutiny as the company’s profit-driven business model frequently clashed with evolving standards for personal information protection and digital security.

### Key Points
- Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt and co-founder Larry Page previously defended the company's data collection practices by distinguishing between user "worry" and actual "harm."
- Google faced a record $22.5 million federal fine for bypassing default privacy settings on Apple’s Safari browsers.
- The company drew criticism for harvesting private Wi-Fi data through its Street View vehicles, prompting investigations into its data collection methods.
- European regulators challenged Google’s privacy policy updates, arguing that the changes violated regional data protection laws.

### Why it Matters
These historical controversies highlight the ongoing tension between the data-dependent business models of major technology firms and the increasing global demand for individual privacy rights. The debate underscores how corporate data practices continue to shape regulatory scrutiny and public trust in the digital economy.

articles.title: DeepMind's CEO says STEM students can use AI 10 times more effectively
articles.author: sgoel@insider.com (Shubhangi Goel)
articles.content:
AI is changing tech careers, but it won't take away the importance of a STEM degree, says Demis Hassabis.
In an interview at a London business conference, the video of which was published on Wednesday, the DeepMind CEO said that knowing the fundamentals of software can give you a leg up in using AI.
"You absolutely needed to lean into STEM and computer science," Hassabis said. "It's just a higher-level programming language is the way you can think about what programming is going to become."
Hassabis, who cofounded DeepMind in 2010, which Google acquired in 2014, said that people first used machine code, then C, then Python. But the future of programming languages may be English.
"You're still going to need to know about architecting things and best software engineering practices," he said. "Those people who understand the deep technical, they'll be able to use these tools 10 times more effectively than people who don't have that technical knowledge."
The DeepMind CEO said AI also creates the need for people to study ethics and social sciences.
"I also believe that the time is now for the humanities like philosophy, economics. I think we really need them in the world we're about to enter," he said.
Hassabis is among tech leaders who are quelling fears that degrees like computer science and engineering are becoming redundant because of vibe coding.
In an interview with Business Insider in December, Geoffrey Hinton, the "godfather of AI," highlighted the value of computer science degrees.
"Obviously, just being a competent mid-level programmer is not going to be a career for much longer, because AI can do that," Hinton said. But the value of a CS degree is much more than just coding, which is why he thinks a "CS degree will be valuable for quite a long time."
Max Levchin, the CEO of Affirm, has also said that computer science fundamentals are key to distinguishing good code from "garbage."
"There's a matter of taste and elegance in programming," he said on a podcast earlier this year. "That's certainly important to me as a programmer, and without having a solid foundation in computer science, I wouldn't be able to have that conversation."
articles.summary:
DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis asserts that STEM degrees remain essential for tech careers, arguing that fundamental software knowledge allows professionals to leverage AI tools with significantly greater effectiveness.

### Key Points
- Demis Hassabis describes AI as a high-level programming language that requires a deep understanding of software architecture and engineering best practices.
- The DeepMind co-founder emphasizes that individuals with technical foundations can utilize AI tools ten times more effectively than those without such training.
- Hassabis advocates for the integration of humanities, including philosophy and economics, to address the ethical and social challenges posed by emerging AI technologies.
- Industry leaders like Geoffrey Hinton and Affirm CEO Max Levchin agree that computer science fundamentals are necessary to maintain quality and elegance in programming.
- Experts suggest that while AI may automate mid-level coding tasks, the core principles of computer science remain critical for long-term career viability.

### Why it Matters
This perspective challenges the growing narrative that AI will render traditional technical education obsolete by automating entry-level coding tasks. By framing AI as a force multiplier for skilled engineers, industry leaders are signaling that the future of tech will prioritize high-level architectural expertise over basic syntax proficiency.

articles.title: Bitcoin under $64,000 after new U.S. strike on Iran. Trump's China comment adds to uncertainty
articles.author: Omkar Godbole
articles.content:
Bitcoin under $63,000 after new U.S. strike on Iran. Trump's China comment adds to uncertainty
Geopolitical tensions and renewed fears of U.S.-China frictions are weighing on risk assets, including bitcoin.
- Bitcoin fell after fresh U.S. airstrikes on Iran weighed on risk sentiment.
- Asian stocks registered losses, with Japan’s Nikkei falling by nearly 3%.
- The Australian dollar, a risk proxy, weakened after President Donald Trump alleged Chinese interference in the U.S. election.
Bitcoin 
BTC, the leading cryptocurrency by market value, slipped below $63,000, extending Thursday’s nearly 1.4% slide from $65,000, according to CoinDesk data. As of this writing, the cryptocurrency traded just below its 50-day simple moving average, the widely-tracked gauge of near-term momentum.
Asian equity markets wilted, with Japan’s Nikkei trading nearly 3% lower at its lowest in over a month. Australia’s ASX 200 slipped by 0.5% alongside a 0.8% drop in futures tied to Nasdaq. Wall Street’s tech-heavy index fell by over 1.6% on Thursday.
Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency quoted Hormozgan Province Governorate, saying that U.S. airstrikes have hit five bridges in the southern Hormozgan province. A missile strike also hit Iran’s Chabahar maritime control tower. Surprisingly, WTI oil futures held steady at around $79 per barrel, ignoring the geopolitical stress from the fresh wave of U.S. attacks on Iran.
In the meantime, the Aussie dollar, a commodity-sensitive currency and G7 proxy for China, fell against the U.S. dollar on fears of renewed U.S.-China tensions.
Late Thursday, Trump announced the declassification of intelligence reports alleging Chinese interference in U.S. elections. He claimed that Beijing obtained 220 million U.S. voter records, calling it a major threat to democracy. China's embassy has flatly denied the allegations.
The dispute itself isn’t market-moving, but its potential to strain U.S.-China ties ahead of Trump’s September meeting with Xi is likely what’s keeping AUD traders on edge.
“Trump's decision to level fresh, sweeping accusations against Beijing weeks ahead of that meeting introduces a new source of friction risk into a relationship that had been steadying,” InvestingLive’s Chief Asia-Pacific Currency Analyst Eamonn Sheridan said in a market update.
“The rhetoric itself could complicate the diplomatic runway into September regardless of the underlying facts,” Sheridan added.
AUD’s drop may be a warning that any escalation in U.S.-China tensions could heighten uncertainty and spill over into other risk assets, including BTC.

articles.title: EU Forces Google To Share Search Data, Open Android To Rivals
articles.author: BeauHD
articles.content: The EU is imposing new rules requiring Google to share anonymized search data and open up Android to rival AI companies. "Thanks to these measures, we hope to see emerging alternatives to Google Search and Google's AI services, such as Gemini, and that users in the EU can enjoy greater choice of services," Henna Virkkunen, an executive vice president at the European Commission overseeing tech, said. The Associated Press reports:  In issuing the two new rules, the commission said it found that AI agents not made by Google were unable to function on Android phones at the same level as Google's Gemini. Google must now allow voice-activation of these alternative AI agents and enable them to run background tasks like booking restaurants via third-party apps. By January 2027, Google must also begin sharing anonymized search data with some rivals. The commission said the move is meant to level the playing field since Google controls a vast trove of user data that no competitor can match.  Google argues the measures could weaken privacy and security by exposing user searches and reducing safeguards around third-party AI assistants. "Europeans' private searches would be exposed to unfamiliar companies, without adequate anonymization of the data and without user knowledge or consent," said Kent Walker, president of global affairs for Google and Alphabet. "This would weaken citizens' privacy, risk business trade secrets, and endanger national security."
articles.summary:
The European Commission is mandating that Google open its Android platform to rival AI developers and share anonymized search data to foster competition within the European Union market.

### Key Points
- Google must enable third-party AI agents to perform background tasks and voice-activation on Android devices at the same level as its Gemini service.
- The European Commission requires Google to begin sharing anonymized search data with competing companies by January 2027.
- EU executive vice president Henna Virkkunen stated the measures aim to provide users with more service choices and reduce Google's dominance.
- Google officials argue the new rules could compromise user privacy, expose sensitive search data, and create potential national security risks.

### Why it Matters
These regulations represent a significant shift in how the EU enforces competition laws against major tech platforms by targeting the underlying data advantages of AI services. The outcome will likely set a global precedent for how dominant companies must share proprietary data and platform access with smaller competitors.

articles.title: US stocks today: Nasdaq ends lower as chip weakness offsets solid earnings, economic data
articles.author: Reuters
articles.content:
Listen to this article in summarized format
Among the 11 major sectors in the S&P 500, technology was one of the biggest percentage losers, with semiconductor stocks weighing heavily on the broader market.
Daily moves in chips have increasingly dictated the overall movement of the major U.S. stock indexes, particularly the tech-heavy Nasdaq.
"It comes strictly down to the weight of the chips in the S&P 500," said Paul Nolte, senior wealth advisor & market strategist at Murphy & Sylvest in Elmhurst, Illinois. "Three or four years ago, it was 8%, and now it's over 20%. If you look at the rest of the market, it's doing fine."
The weakness in chips, even after chip demand bellwether TSMC posted a 77% jump in quarterly profit, demonstrated the lofty expectations for a sector that has soared by nearly 70% so far this year. U.S.-listed shares of the chipmaker lost ground on the day.
Memory-chip makers were among the biggest laggards, with SanDisk, Western Digital, Seagate Technology , and Intel among the largest percentage losers.
The Dow's losses were cushioned in part by UnitedHealth Group's advance after the company beat Wall Street earnings estimates and hiked its 2026 forecast.
United Airlines fell as surging oil prices weighed on its forward guidance.
GE Aerospace slid even after the company lifted its 2026 profit forecast.
Analysts have set a high bar for second-quarter earnings season. S&P 500 companies, in aggregate, are expected to post year-on-year earnings growth of 24.8%. Technology earnings alone are seen jumping 65.5% from the year-ago quarter, according to the latest available data from LSEG.
SOLID RETAIL SALES, LOW JOBLESS CLAIMS, WEAK HOUSING DATA
A spate of U.S.economic indicators released on Thursday showed solid core retail sales, a drop in jobless claims and surging manufacturing activity in the Northeast.
Less positive data came from the housing sector, with a bigger than expected drop in pending home sales and souring homebuilder sentiment reflecting high borrowing costs and strained affordability for would-be homebuyers.
The U.S. and Iran extended their barrage of airstrikes, prolonging a week-long escalation that has all but voided last month's truce. But Iran's release of a U.S. citizen suggested a path remains for the two sides to avert the resumption of all-out war.

articles.title: Infinix HOT 70 Series Review: Designed Like a Flagship, Not Priced Like One
articles.author: JC Torres
articles.content:
PROS:
- Eye-catching designs that deliver a strong visual identity
- Color-changing Thermo Orange opens DIY possibilities
- Clous de Paris-inspired button makes AI more tactile and personal
- Decent performance and strong value for its price
CONS:
- No dedicated telephoto or ultra-wide cameras
- Infinix HOT 70 (non-Pro) is limited to 4G networks only
The affordable smartphone market has long operated on an unspoken rule: you get your camera, your processor, and a solid battery, but genuine design ambition is a luxury reserved for pricier tiers. Walk into any mid-range phone aisle, and you’re likely to face the same predictable cycle, gradient blues and purples on glass-and-plastic slabs that look almost indistinguishable from one another, functional but rarely intentional.
Infinix is trying to challenge that assumption with the HOT 70 series with its Industry-First 12-Colorway Dynamic Shine Design. It comes in two distinct flavors: the HOT 70 and the HOT 70 Pro 5G. Both arrived for this review, the HOT 70 in Quiet Violet and the HOT 70 Pro 5G in Thermo Orange and Mirage Green, and all announced themselves as something genuinely
Designer: Infinix
Click Here to Buy Now: HOT 70 | HOT 70 Pro 5G
Aesthetics
Infinix has long understood something that a lot of phone brands still haven’t fully caught on to: young users aren’t shopping for specs alone, they’re buying something that reflects who they are and what they’re about. The HOT 70 series is the brand’s clearest statement on that front, organized around a “Colorful, Versatile, Fun” philosophy that treats aesthetic identity and personal expression as core design priorities, not afterthoughts.
Both phones share a design language Infinix calls the Dynamic Shine Aesthetic Design, and the common thread across both models is a Baby-Smooth Finish, a surface with an advanced anti-fingerprint coating. That silky, finely soft in-hand texture genuinely holds up in practice. After time with both phones, fingerprints and grease smudges are noticeably less of a nuisance than on most others in this price category, which counts for a lot.
Infinix HOT 70
The HOT 70 arrives in six colorways: Thermo Orange, Night Pulse, Silver Dancer, Dive Blue, Green Texture, and the Quiet Violet reviewed here. Where most phones at this price simply pick a color and commit to it, the Quiet Violet does something more considered. The Lumina Flare Dynamic Flame Texture layers micro-textures beneath the surface, creating a shifting light-and-shadow effect that reads differently depending on where you are.
The Crystal Mood Island camera ring is where the craftsmanship gets more deliberate. It sits around the camera bump as a decorative ring that catches light and bends it into iridescent gloss, shifting tone with every viewing angle. On paper, that sounds like a marketing detail, but in practice, it adds genuine visual contrast between the ring and the rest of the panel behind it.
Then there’s the Active Halo, the light ring surrounding the camera area that pulses softly for calls, messages, charging status, and game launches. It’s tempting to dismiss this as a gimmick, but there’s a genuinely useful case for it: the phone is face down, silent, and the Halo quietly signals an incoming call without making you reach for it every few minutes.
Infinix HOT 70 Pro 5G
The HOT 70 Pro 5G is available in six colorways: Thermo Orange, Mirage Green, Silk Glow Purple, Night Pulse, Dive Blue, and the limited edition Depth Ring White. The Thermo Orange commands the most attention, and rightly so. Its Dual-way Thermo Sensing Skin technology physically shifts between a deeper orange below 0°C and a lighter tone above 65°C, and crucially, holds that state until the temperature resets it.
What the Thermo Orange also enables is DIY customization: cool the phone to its deep state, then press a heated stamp or trace with a hot glue pen at around 65°C to create patterns directly onto the back. The concept is genuinely novel for this price tier, and it’s quite fascinating to see the phone come out of the freezer with a different shade of orange. It also opens up possibilities for DIY designs with the stencils and the careful use of a glue gun to heat up only certain areas, creating eye-catching contrasts of light and dark areas you’d think the phone was designed with originally. Given that you are exposing the phone to extreme cold and hot temperatures, however, proper care should still be taken.
Mirage Green, exclusive to the Pro model, offers a more subtle yet equally stunning color-changing effect. Nearly-invisible luminous particles embedded in the leather-like finish on the back of the phone absorb light during the day. Once the sun sets, these dots glow like stars dots scattered against a dark green night sky. It’s a visual that is both premium and calming, perfectly reflecting its design.
The remaining hero finishes deserve more than a footnote. Silk Glow Purple builds its color from nano-scale structural layers rather than pigment, which means the purple actually shifts and travels across the surface as the angle changes. The Limited Edition Depth Ring White goes further, using a micro-lens array to create what reads as a floating 3D ring against an otherwise flat back panel.
The Active Matrix Cube is the Pro’s most distinctive rear-surface detail, a programmable light matrix that’s a flagship-level feature from Infinix’s premium product lineup. It handles status alerts, ambient lighting, mini-games, and custom pattern displays, and can even share those creations via NFC with a single tap. It turns the back of the phone into a secondary surface that communicates something, even when the screen is dark.
Ergonomics
At just 7.49mm thin and 195g light, the HOT 70’s pocket-favourite design offers effortless portability, slipping easily into pockets or small bags and ensuring smooth one-handed handling for commuting, travel, and everyday use. The HOT 70 Pro 5G is marginally heavier at 204g and slightly thicker at 7.85mm to 7.95mm, depending on the colorway, differences that don’t read as particularly significant in everyday handling.
The One-Tap AI Button on both phones is inspired by the Clous de Paris design, with a three-dimensional textured surface that delivers a refined, premium tactile feel. Its customizable shortcut logic enables One-Tap AI FlashMemo with a short press and activates Folax AI assistant with a long press, allowing intuitive control and more efficient smart interaction by streamlining everyday tasks. That physical anchor makes AI feel genuinely accessible rather than buried in software menus.
Both phones use punch-hole displays with narrow bezels, giving a clean, open canvas for everyday use. The HOT 70 and HOT 70 Pro 5G have very similar screen sizes at 6.78 and 6.76 inches, respectively, so the real ergonomic difference lies in the refresh rate. The HOT 70 Pro 5G’s 144Hz high refresh rate and 240Hz touch sampling rate deliver ultra-smooth scrolling without ghosting while browsing and streaming, while the HOT 70’s 120Hz panel handles daily navigation comfortably. The smooth visuals help ease eye strain while also improving the overall user experience.
Performance
AI is quite the trend these days, and Infinix isn’t one to be left behind. The HOT 70 Series delivers a full AI experience upgrade featuring a physical One-Tap AI Button, with a dedicated AI Button that supports intelligent intent recognition, enabling one-tap capture of ideas, meetings, emails, and more, with auto archiving and smart schedule creation based on content type. Long-pressing the button activates Folax, Infinix’s custom AI assistant, which answers questions based on whatever is on screen, from displayed text to a live camera view, with access to five or more AI models simultaneously. Between FlashMemo’s capture-and-organize logic and Folax’s any-screen contextual awareness, the two together cover most of what people actually reach for their phones to do throughout the day.
Battery anxiety is real, especially among the younger generation, so Infinix naturally has a solution. Both models offer a 15% larger battery capacity than the previous generation, featuring a 6,000 mAh single-cell design or 5,600 mAh in certain regional markets, a capacity difference driven by local regulatory requirements rather than any hardware compromise. Fortunately, both capacities support 45W Lightning FastCharge, and in Super Fast Charging mode, which allowed us to hit 50% in half an hour and fully charge in an hour. Bypass charging routes power directly from the charger to keep the phone cooler during heavy use, a critical safety measure when gaming or using the phone for navigation.
The Infinix HOT 70 runs on a MediaTek Helio G100 Ultimate, a powerful octa-core gaming processor designed for smoother daily use, multitasking, and light gaming. That said, the Helio G100 is a 4G-only chip, which means the HOT 70 can’t connect to 5G networks. The HOT 70 Pro 5G steps up to a MediaTek Dimensity 7100 with 5G support, a meaningfully stronger chip that pushes AnTuTu V11 scores above 780,000, delivers up to 90 fps in supported games, and brings faster 5G connectivity tuned for crowded real-world conditions.
The display difference between the two models is more significant than the size gap suggests. The HOT 70’s 6.78-inch 120Hz Eye-Care Display reaches 700 nits of HBM brightness for sunlight readability. The HOT 70 Pro 5G’s 6.76-inch FHD+ panel pushes that to 950 nits of HBM brightness, a genuine sunlight-legibility advantage, and the jump to Full HD+ resolution adds sharpness that becomes particularly noticeable when reading text or watching video.
Camera setups share a foundation but diverge meaningfully at the Pro tier. Both phones use a 50MP main sensor, with AI Live Photo on both automatically filtering out shaky frames from a burst. The HOT 70 Pro 5G’s imaging toolkit, however, rests on a 50MP Sony IMX882 with a large 1/1.95-inch sensor, f/1.8 aperture, and full-pixel autofocus, plus a 2x lossless zoom shortcut, giving it stronger low-light performance.
In both cases, both images and videos come out clear, detailed, and vibrant, punching above their price tier. Sadly, the lack of a dedicated telephoto or ultra-wide cameras will make you miss out on decent close-ups and breath-taking panoramas. It isn’t the series’s strongest suit, but it at least won’t let you down when you need to preserve important moments and scenes.
Sustainability
The HOT 70 carries an IP65 rating, featuring six-level full dustproofing and IP65 splash resistance, blocking dust, rain, and sweat effectively for safer daily commutes and outdoor use. It also boasts military-grade durability with an SGS-certified 5-star drop protection. The HOT 70 Pro 5G steps up to IP68, Infinix’s highest rating to date, rated for up to 2 meters of fresh water for 30 minutes, with screen and camera protected throughout, and further adds MIL-STD-810 military-grade certification for durability across more demanding conditions.
Both phones deliver long-term OS support, keeping the device smooth, secure, and reliable for years, with guaranteed three years of major XOS version upgrades (only for models with 6GB RAM or more) starting with factory-installed XOS 16, continuously updating to XOS 17, XOS 18, and XOS 19. Three OS upgrades aren’t quite on par with what some flagship brands now offer, but it’s a genuinely generous commitment for this price tier, where software longevity is rarely treated as a selling point at all. One caveat is that certain XOS features only apply if your configuration has 6GB of RAM or more.
Value
The Infinix HOT 70 starts at around $120, depending on configuration and market, and the package it delivers at that price is hard to dismiss. Tactile finishes, a physical AI shortcut, a generous battery, and a sunlight-readable display aren’t common at this tier. The Lumina Flare texture and Crystal Mood Island add visual differentiation that most competitors simply don’t bother attempting.
The Infinix HOT 70 Pro 5G starts at around $200, and the step up from the base HOT 70 is justified on multiple fronts. A faster 5G chip, a sharper and faster display, a more capable Sony camera sensor, full IP68 waterproofing alongside military certification, and the Active Matrix Cube add up to a meaningfully more capable and expressive package.
Neither phone asks for a premium price to deliver a premium-feeling design, and that’s genuinely rare. At comparable price points, the alternatives in this category tend to compete primarily on camera megapixels or battery size, without investing much thought into what the phone feels like to hold, look at, or interact with. The HOT 70 series treats those questions as worth answering, which puts it in a different conversation entirely.
Verdict
The HOT 70 makes a case for what affordable phone design can look like when treated as an actual discipline. The Lumina Flare texture, the Crystal Mood Island camera ring, the Active Halo, and the Baby-Smooth Finish come together in a device that rewards closer attention. It gets noticed on a table or out of a pocket, not because it’s loud, but because it clearly has a point of view. The HOT 70 Pro 5G takes that design ambition further into something genuinely experimental, with four optical behaviors across its colorways, a 5G chip with real gaming headroom, a Sony camera sensor that performs above expectations, and IP68 waterproofing for more demanding conditions.
Together, both phones say something interesting about where considered, expressive design is heading at the more accessible end of the smartphone market. At a price tier where “design” usually means picking between black, blue, or midnight black, the Infinix HOT 70 series is quietly raising the bar for what a phone at this price is allowed to look and feel like.
Click Here to Buy Now: HOT 70 | HOT 70 Pro 5G
articles.summary:
The new Infinix HOT 70 and HOT 70 Pro 5G smartphones prioritize expressive, tactile design and AI integration to challenge the standard aesthetic limitations of the affordable mobile market.

### Key Points
- The HOT 70 series features unique finishes, including color-shifting Thermo Orange and luminous Mirage Green, alongside a tactile "Clous de Paris" inspired AI button.
- The HOT 70 Pro 5G includes a 144Hz display, a 50MP Sony IMX882 camera sensor, and IP68 water resistance, while the base model is limited to 4G connectivity.
- Both devices house large 6,000 mAh batteries with 45W fast charging and offer three years of major XOS software upgrades for models with 6GB of RAM or more.
- The Pro model utilizes a MediaTek Dimensity 7100 5G chip, whereas the standard HOT 70 is powered by the 4G-only MediaTek Helio G100 Ultimate processor.
- Pricing starts at approximately $120 for the HOT 70 and $200 for the HOT 70 Pro 5G, positioning them as design-focused alternatives in the budget segment.

### Why it Matters
By focusing on premium-feeling materials and experimental aesthetics, Infinix is attempting to shift the budget smartphone category away from generic, utilitarian designs. This approach provides younger, cost-conscious consumers with high-end visual features and AI-driven functionality that are typically reserved for significantly more expensive flagship devices.

articles.title: Proxmox is overkill for a single mini PC, and that's exactly why I put it on mine
articles.author: Rich Edmonds
articles.content:
The mini PC was always lumped in with netbooks and NUCs. They were great for getting basic work done, but as soon as you'd command one to do anything slightly more demanding, they would choke under their own weight. That's no longer the case, as modern mini PCs, even more affordable ones, come rocking more powerful Intel and AMD processors, allowing you to easily work and game on a device more compact than a box of tissues. This is how I can run Proxmox on a few mini PCs to create the ultimate low-power home lab cluster.
A single mini PC is still bare-bones
It's no AMD Ryzen Threadripper...
There's no escaping the law of physics. A compact mini PC just isn't going to be as powerful as a full-blown mid-tower server PC, nor will it destroy rack systems in benchmark tests. But what a mini PC will do is silently keep out of your way. It'll hum along, sipping power from the outlet like the most British of Brits holding a cup of tea on a hot sunny day. Even a small system with an N100 CPU can easily run Proxmox and a few containers. It's all about how you position the home lab and take advantage of all available system resources.
Proxmox being completely overkill for a mini PC isn't a knock on the form factor. It's actually why I love using Proxmox. It's intentionally overengineered, so much so that just about anyone can go from not knowing what network-attached storage (NAS) is to having a full self-hosted stack and compelling smart home in no time. For many, using a mini PC would warrant Debian, Ubuntu Server, or some other lightweight Linux distro to better make use of the less capable hardware, but there's also another route that includes Proxmox.
Instead of settling with a small single system running a few containers, such as Pi-hole, Jellyfin, Home Assistant, and maybe Immich if you're feeling even just slightly adventurous, I decided to throw Proxmox onto an old mini PC I had lying around collecting dust. This was whilst I had a beefy running with 64 cores and just about everything the household required. Frigate, Mealie, Immich, Nextcloud, and much more were all happily running on the same Proxmox node. But as soon as Proxmox went on this Minisforum U850, I knew I was onto something.
Sure, the U850 isn't packing a low-power N100, nor was it the most affordable system available at the time, but it's getting on a little bit now, and the Core i5-10210U is a moderately capable chip. Joining 16GB of DDR4 RAM, Intel UHD 630 graphics, a 250GB NVMe SSD, and a single 2.5GbE port, it was primed for running a few containers.
What makes Proxmox ultimately overkill
It's all under the hood
Instead of installing Debian and calling it a day, Proxmox gives you so much more. It's a hypervisor at the core, offering the means for me to launch a virtual machine (VM) running and lightweight LXCs for Pi-hole, Homepage, and other self-hosted apps. But what Proxmox really allowed me to do was get creative and try something new. Experimenting with a Proxmox node or cluster is incredibly straightforward. It's as simple as creating a virtual instance, messing around, and either destroying it when done or launching it on the LAN.
Honestly, it makes running a home lab easier than it ought to be, all things considered.
Another great use for Proxmox and self-hosting is the snapshots. These backups are amazingly powerful, making migration and recovery seamless. With a few clicks, I can have even more complex self-hosted apps back up and running in no time. Bare metal starts to feel sticky once you've tested Proxmox. Gone are the days when I'd need to add packages to the underlying host system and ensure everything had the right prerequisites already installed. I recall fixing one thing and breaking another with a separate app. That's no longer the case.
Proxmox lets me keep even the less capable systems running smoothly with clear dashboard metrics and other telemetry to know just how much is being directed to running instances. If I'm no longer requiring a specific VM, I can safely delete it. If a container ceases to work and I'm done trying, I can roll it back to an earlier snapshot, and all the different apps I'm running are all happy with specific packages that don't interfere with anything else live on the same box. Honestly, it makes running a home lab easier than it ought to be, all things considered.
The web UI is something else
I honestly can't count the times I've relied on snapshots to get me out of a sticky situation, and this is something I may have struggled with using a "normal" bare-metal Linux distro. But the Proxmox web UI is really something else. Sure, SSH exists and is more than convenient for those with the know-how to get in, complete a task, and drop the connection in record time, but not everyone can achieve this, which is where the Proxmox web interface comes into play. It makes the entire system visible—actually, it makes the entire home lab visible.
Have a few mini PCs? Run them as Proxmox nodes within the same cluster, and a single web UI can be used to manage the entire stack. It's stupidly powerful and makes managing everything a breeze. But all that power and capability does come at a cost. Proxmox isn't free to run. You don't have to pay for the license, but it'll suck up valuable system resources, especially when running on a more baseline mini PC. It's overkill for running a single VM and LXC, for instance.
But this is a tradeoff worth making, at least for my home lab needs. I get to rely on less power-hungry hardware such as mini PCs and compact business desktop systems. VMs can be dedicated to more powerful applications with LXCs at the ready for more basic apps. Storage paths can be separated and snapshots taken for the ultimate local recovery strategy. Remote storage, namely my TrueNAS network storage, can be added with a few clicks and you're good to go.
Proxmox is overkill, but that's what makes it so good
Proxmox really is something else. It takes what is essentially a single mini PC and transforms it from running Windows to managing an entire home. It's capable, powerful, and completely free, making Proxmox not only my go-to for running various systems and the like, but also one I would always recommend to those looking to create a home lab. Bare-metal installs are great for a single app or two, but once you're ready to leap, Proxmox will run well so long as you have a decent enough CPU and RAM.
Proxmox
Proxmox is an open-source platform built on Debian Linux designed for server virtualization.
articles.summary:
Modern mini PCs equipped with powerful Intel and AMD processors are increasingly capable of running Proxmox, enabling users to build efficient, low-power home lab clusters for self-hosted applications.

### Key Points
- Modern mini PCs, including those with N100 CPUs, provide sufficient performance to run virtualization platforms like Proxmox.
- Proxmox allows users to manage multiple virtual machines and lightweight LXC containers from a single, centralized web interface.
- Key features like system snapshots and backups simplify recovery and prevent software conflicts common in bare-metal Linux installations.
- Users can cluster multiple mini PCs together to create a scalable, energy-efficient home lab environment for services like Jellyfin or Home Assistant.
- The platform is open-source and built on Debian Linux, offering enterprise-grade virtualization tools for compact, consumer-grade hardware.

### Why it Matters
Using Proxmox on compact hardware lowers the barrier to entry for building sophisticated, self-hosted home networks and smart home systems. This approach allows enthusiasts to maximize the utility of affordable, energy-efficient devices while maintaining the flexibility and reliability of professional-grade server management.

articles.title: The new AI-enhanced Siri is quietly revolutionary. Here’s why
articles.author: Ben Patterson
articles.content:
Summary created by Smart Answers AI
In summary:
- PCWorld reports that Apple’s new AI-enhanced Siri, now in public beta, takes a revolutionary business-like approach focused on practical tasks rather than companion-style interaction.
- The updated Siri efficiently organizes emails, retrieves specific information, and brainstorms ideas without attempting to bond with users like ChatGPT or Claude.
- This shift toward utility-focused AI assistance will reach wider audiences with the fall release of iOS 27 and macOS 27.
After a couple years of false starts, the new AI-enhanced Siri has finally arrived on Apple devices via public beta. Its most revolutionary feature may be what it doesn’t do – namely, it doesn’t try to be your buddy.
I’ve been testing the new Siri (which goes into wide release this fall once iOS 27, macOS 27, and the Apple’s other OS 27’s land) on my iPhone for the past few days, and I’m struck by how business-like it is. 
No, the new Siri isn’t curt or blunt, but it’s not trying to cozy up to me, either. It’s not showering me with praise, nor is it needlessly trying to extend conversations. It just does what you ask it to do (well, aside from a few bugs), then gets out of the way. Saying “Here you go” after it completes a task is about as effusive as the new Siri gets.
Siri’s lack of suck-upage shouldn’t be confused with a lack of AI ability. The revamped Siri can do some pretty cool things, from sifting through and organizing email to brainstorming ideas, putting them into a Notes file, and then tucking that file into any folder you see fit. 
			
		
Welcome to another edition of Prompt Mode, your weekly AI newsletter.
I’m your host, Ben Patterson. Each week on Prompt Mode, I’ll be serving up analysis of the AI trends that matter to everyday users like you and me. Stay tuned for practical AI tips, hands-on experiences with the latest AI tools, and–you guessed it–prompts to help you get the most out of your AI assistants.
Thanks for reading, and if you like what you see, just sign up right here.
 
In another nifty example, I asked Siri about my daughter’s class field trip this morning. Siri was able to pluck the info from my email and text messages, telling me where and when the meetup was, along with the teacher’s phone number. That’s the kind of practical, everyday AI help that I could really use.
Most importantly, when I’m done with Siri, it disappears. The new Siri may be happy to help, but it’s not a friend, nor is it the star of the show.
Apple’s understated approach with Siri stands in stark contrast with what we’re seeing with ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. They often feel like they’re trying to bond with us or even pass for human.
Indeed, there was a report earlier this week that OpenAI’s rumored ChatGPT smart speaker is designed to “feel like a companion” and “connect on a humanlike level with users.” (OpenAI hasn’t commented on the report.)
The new Siri, on the other hand, doesn’t feel like a companion or confidant. From what I’ve seen so far, it’s just a competent AI assistant. And that’s what it should be.
More in AI this week
- Now powered by OpenAI’s latest model, the revamped ChatGPT app can code, control your PC, browse the web, and even publish websites. (PCWorld)
- Wait, the new ChatGPT can control your computer? It sure can, and I tested it by asking ChatGPT to play chess and write a journal entry. Here’s how it went. (PCWorld)
- OpenAI’s first hardware product is here, and it’s … a mini keyboard for Codex hotkeys. It can be yours for $230. Pass. (Business Insider)
- Meta wisely yanked that icky AI feature that let users deepface public Instagram photos. (TechCrunch)
- Anthropic and OpenAI are loosening usage limits for Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol, their latest and most powerful models. Indeed,  usage limits for Claude and GPT models were just reset again earlier this week. Let ‘er rip! (PCWorld)
Prompt of the week: The “pencil and paper” prompt
So, you asked Claude Code to diagnose a bug in your new Windows app, and it just took off and started coding. Sound familiar? It’s happened to me plenty of times, and while I appreciate Claude Code’s initiative, sometimes I’d rather talk things through first.
That’s where the “pencil and paper” prompt comes in handy. Whenever I ask Claude Code, Codex, or Google’s Antigravity a question, but want to keep it from galloping ahead, I add this to my prompt: “Stay in pencil and paper mode.” It’s a handy way to keep Claude, Codex, and Antigravity in check, and it works for other applications besides coding, too.
That’s all for now!
Thanks for reading the latest issue of Prompt Mode. Want more next week? Don’t forget to sign up to start receiving this newsletter in your inbox.
articles.summary:
Apple has launched a public beta for its revamped, utility-focused Siri, which prioritizes efficient task completion over conversational companionship ahead of a wider release in iOS 27 and macOS 27.

### Key Points
- The updated Siri is currently available to users through a public beta program.
- New capabilities include organizing emails, retrieving specific information from messages, and brainstorming ideas for Notes.
- Unlike competitors such as ChatGPT or Claude, the assistant avoids human-like bonding or extended conversational filler.
- The official wide release is scheduled for this fall alongside the launch of iOS 27, macOS 27, and other Apple operating systems.

### Why it Matters
Apple’s shift toward a strictly functional AI assistant highlights a growing industry divide between tools designed for companionship and those built for productivity. This approach may appeal to users who prefer efficient, unobtrusive software over AI models that attempt to mimic human social interaction.

articles.title: Netflix Co-CEO Explains How Gen-AI Was Used in 300 Different Titles: ‘We Believe It Is Going to Enhance Their Abilities’
articles.author: Brian Welk
articles.content:
By providing your information, you agree to our Terms of Use and our Privacy Policy. We use vendors that may also process your information to help provide our services. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA Enterprise and the Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service apply.
Netflix disclosed in its Q2 Earnings call on Thursday that generative AI has been used in 300 of its titles, including in the early creative process and in post-production to enhance shots that it says could not have been accomplished otherwise.
The detail about the 300 titles was disclosed in the letter to shareholders, and Co-CEO Ted Sarandos elaborated on that, explaining that in the case of the recent “The American Experiment” docuseries, 17 minutes of the show has AI-enhanced footage. Sarandos said many of the shows have used AI tools for pre-vis, VFX, set references, and post-production, but also in the case of things like enhancing crowd shots or historical battle scenes.
“It enabled us to expand the scope of the series in ways that wouldn’t have been feasible before,” Sarandos said on the call of “The American Experiment. “Those 17 minutes, they were produced twice as fast and at half the cost of previous options. By equipping creatives with these tools, we believe it is going to enhance their abilities and have better and more impact for every dollar we spend on our programming.”
It’s a full-throated embrace of AI after Netflix acquired Ben Affleck’s InterPositive AI company earlier this year, but Sarandos clarified that it’s still early with InterPositive and these are the product of other tools that are all “working together to drive innovation.” The letter to shareholders mentions a handful of other titles that he said used AI, including “Glory” (India) and “Brasil 70: A Saga do Tri” (Brazil).
“Keep in mind that in many of the cases, productions would’ve left out those key shots because they wouldn’t have been able to afford them, they wouldn’t have been able to do them in the time frames that they’re working on,” Sarandos elaborated. “So those sequences are saved by the availability and access to the Gen-AI tools.”
Sarandos did still defend the artistry that goes into each film and reiterated that these are tools for filmmakers to use, but the examples he’s describing today are “just the beginning,” and there are use cases that are “scaling faster and faster.”
“On the content side, we believe it takes great art to make something great, and AI is not changing that,” he said. “AI will give creatives better tools to bring their visions to life. Movies are being made by people who make movies. AI provides them with better tools to make them even better.”
The question about AI was about how the addition of InterPositive could impact the overall $20 billion content spend for Netflix and whether any savings from AI would feed back into other content or into paying talent, and Sarandos’ short answer was yes.
“Content creation timelines can be shortened, and quality can be enhanced, so the cost savings will likely be re-invested into more content on the service, which fuels high quality engagement, and that whole revenue-profit flywheel is going to come from that that we’ve been talking about from Day 1.”
Sarandos has previously addressed the use of AI more as a planning tool, saying in response to a James Cameron quote that there’s “an even bigger opportunity if you can make movies 10 percent better.” But the company has never put a number to the amount of projects that have utilized AI and have actual shots enhanced by generative AI within them, especially considering none of those series have previously been disclosed as containing AI.
Other studios such as Amazon have been vocal about actual instances in which AI has been used to enhance their programming, though there was intense backlash to one animation creator whose show was greenlit after it was disclosed he employed AI to make it.
Former Netflix CEO Reed Hastings also recently joined the board of AI giant Anthropic.
articles.summary:
Netflix revealed during its Q2 earnings call that generative AI tools have been utilized in 300 titles to enhance production quality, reduce costs, and accelerate creative timelines.

### Key Points
- Netflix used generative AI in 300 titles for pre-visualization, visual effects, and post-production enhancements.
- The docuseries "The American Experiment" featured 17 minutes of AI-enhanced footage produced at half the cost and twice the speed of traditional methods.
- Co-CEO Ted Sarandos confirmed the company acquired Ben Affleck’s InterPositive AI earlier this year to further drive production innovation.
- Other international titles utilizing these tools include the Indian film "Glory" and the Brazilian production "Brasil 70: A Saga do Tri."
- Netflix plans to reinvest cost savings from AI-driven efficiencies into producing more high-quality content for its global subscriber base.

### Why it Matters
The integration of generative AI into Netflix's production pipeline signals a significant shift in how major studios manage content budgets and creative workflows. By scaling these tools, Netflix aims to improve production value while reinvesting savings to fuel its long-term revenue and profit growth.

articles.title: Grok CLI Secretly Uploaded Full User Directories and SSH Keys
articles.author: Julian Horsey
articles.content:
The Grok Command-Line Interface (CLI), developed by xAI, has faced significant backlash after reports revealed that it uploaded entire user directories to its servers without explicit consent. Investigations uncovered that sensitive data, including SSH keys, Git repositories and password manager databases, were transmitted even when users disabled data-sharing features. This behavior, which persisted due to invasive default settings, has drawn comparisons to malware and raised serious concerns about xAI’s transparency and commitment to privacy. Better Stack highlights how these revelations have sparked broader discussions about the ethical responsibilities of AI companies in handling user data.
Gain insight into the specific steps xAI has taken to address these privacy concerns, such as introducing a `/privacy` command and disabling certain data-sharing flags. Explore how Grok’s practices compare to competitors like Claude Code and Gemini, which prioritize stricter data protection measures. Additionally, learn actionable strategies for safeguarding your own data while using Grok, from reviewing logs to configuring stricter privacy settings. These insights provide a clearer understanding of the challenges and potential solutions in balancing innovation with user trust.
Unauthorized Data Uploads: A Breach of Trust?
Investigations have revealed that Grok’s default settings facilitated the automatic upload of entire directories, including sensitive files, to xAI servers. This behavior persisted even when users opted out of data-sharing by disabling the “help improve this model” feature. Critical data such as Git repository histories, SSH keys and environment variables were transmitted without user consent. These revelations have drawn sharp criticism from both users and security experts, who argue that such practices represent a significant breach of trust. The lack of transparency in Grok’s design and its invasive default settings have raised serious concerns about the tool’s commitment to user privacy.
How Security Experts Uncovered the Issue
Security researchers employed advanced tools, including Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) proxies, to analyze Grok’s data transmission patterns. Their investigations confirmed that Grok uploaded entire repositories and associated metadata covertly, even when users attempted to restrict access. This behavior has drawn comparisons to malware due to its stealthy and invasive nature. The findings have exposed fundamental flaws in Grok’s architecture, highlighting its failure to adhere to established privacy standards. These revelations have intensified scrutiny of xAI’s practices and raised broader questions about the ethical responsibilities of technology companies in handling user data.
Expand your understanding of Grok AI with additional resources from our extensive library of articles.
- Grok 4.3 Features Most Beginners Overlook : Unlock Smarter AI Answers
- 10 Free AI Video Tools Tested : Only 3 Actually Deliver
- Inside the Grok 5 Roadmap: How xAI Plans to Reach AGI
- Grok 4.5 Fully Tested: Matches GPT 5.5 Coding for $2 per Million Input Tokens
- How To Use Grok 4 AI : Personas, Workflows, Creativity and More
- Grok 3 Review : How Does It Compare to ChatGPT, Claude and Google Gemini?
- How to create consistent characters with AI – FLUX and LORA for images and Kling AI for video
- Grok 5 : The Shocking AGI Breakthrough No One’s Talking About
- Grok’s Bias To Musk Explored : Who Shapes the Truth in AI?
- Grok 4.2 Beginner Guide : Reasoning Traces & Supports Source Priority for Research
How Grok Compares to Competitors
In the competitive landscape of AI-powered coding tools, Grok’s approach to data handling stands out for its lack of privacy safeguards. Competitors such as Claude Code, Code X and Gemini have implemented stricter protocols to ensure user data remains secure. These tools are designed to upload only files directly relevant to specific user prompts, minimizing the risk of exposing sensitive information. In contrast, Grok’s invasive data-sharing practices have highlighted a significant gap in its privacy measures. This disparity has fueled criticism of xAI and underscored the importance of robust data protection standards in the development of AI tools.
xAI’s Response to the Backlash
Faced with mounting criticism, xAI has introduced several updates aimed at addressing Grok’s privacy issues. Key changes include disabling the “trace upload” flag, adding a “disable code base upload” setting and implementing a `/privacy` command to manage data retention policies. Elon Musk, the founder of xAI, announced that all previously uploaded data would be deleted from the company’s servers. Despite these efforts, concerns remain about the underlying mechanisms that enable data uploads. The reliance on server-side flags to control data-sharing behavior has left many users skeptical about the effectiveness of these measures and xAI’s commitment to transparency.
Lingering Privacy Concerns
The newly introduced `/privacy` command, while a step forward, operates as a server-side toggle. This means it does not prevent data transmission from the client side, requiring users to manually enable privacy settings for each session. This approach has complicated usability and raised further questions about xAI’s dedication to protecting user data. The reliance on server-side controls has deepened skepticism about the company’s transparency and its ability to safeguard sensitive information. Until these issues are fully resolved, doubts about Grok’s privacy practices are likely to persist.
Steps You Can Take to Protect Your Data
If you are a Grok CLI user, there are proactive measures you can take to enhance your data security:
- Regularly review logs to detect any unauthorized data transmissions.
- Rotate sensitive credentials, such as SSH keys, to mitigate potential risks.
- Disable features within Grok that could lead to unintended data uploads.
- Follow detailed guides to configure Grok CLI with stricter privacy settings.
By taking these steps, you can reduce the risk of unauthorized data exposure while xAI works to address the tool’s privacy shortcomings.
Trust in Grok and xAI Remains Fragile
Despite xAI’s efforts to mitigate the privacy concerns surrounding Grok, the core functionality allowing data uploads remains embedded in the tool. The reliance on server-side flags to control data-sharing behavior continues to undermine user confidence. Many users remain unconvinced that xAI has taken sufficient steps to ensure transparency and protect sensitive information. Until these underlying issues are fully addressed, trust in Grok CLI and xAI’s broader commitment to user privacy is likely to remain fragile. The incident serves as a stark reminder of the importance of prioritizing privacy in the development of AI tools.

articles.summary:
The xAI Grok Command-Line Interface is facing intense scrutiny after reports revealed the tool automatically uploaded sensitive user directories and private credentials to company servers without explicit consent.

### Key Points

- Unauthorized data uploads occurred even when users disabled "help improve this model" settings, drawing comparisons to malware behavior.

articles.title: Samsung launches 990 SSD with up to 2TB capacity in India
articles.author: Asif Iqbal Shaik
articles.content:
Last updated: July 17th, 2026 at 09:33 UTC+02:00
It is a cheaper SSD compared to other models from Samsung but still offers top-notch speeds.
Samsung
Amid the ongoing memory and storage chip shortage, Samsung has launched a new SSD in India. The 990 SSD is almost as fast as the company's higher-priced SSDs, but it uses 4-bit QLC NAND flash instead of TLC NAND. This makes it cheaper, although it has lower endurance.
The Samsung 990 SSD is a PCIe 4.0 drive with sequential read and write speeds of up to 7,250MB/s and 6,450MB/s, respectively. It is available in 1TB and 2TB models, priced at INR 25,649 (~$266) and INR 50,349 (~$523), respectively. The SSD will be available for purchase in India starting in the last week of July. It uses the M.2 2280 form factor.
The SSD is primarily designed for secondary data storage, budget setups, or secondary game libraries. The 1TB model has an endurance rating of 400TBW, while the 2TB version is rated for 800TBW. That makes its endurance 33% lower TBW than the 990 Evo, 990 Evo Plus, and 990 Pro SSDs.
Samsung
The SSD comes with Samsung Magician software and offers AES 256-bit full-disk encryption, TCG/Opal V2.0, and Encrypted Drive (IEEE 1667) security features. It also has a three-year limited warranty.
Asif is a computer engineer turned technology journalist. He has been using Samsung phones since 2004, and his current smartphone is the Galaxy S23 Ultra. He loves headphones, mechanical keyboards, and PC hardware. When not writing about technology, he likes watching crime and science fiction movies and TV shows.
articles.summary:
Samsung has launched the new 990 SSD in India, offering high-speed PCIe 4.0 performance at a lower price point by utilizing 4-bit QLC NAND flash memory technology.

### Key Points
- The drive delivers sequential read speeds of 7,250MB/s and write speeds of 6,450MB/s.
- Available in 1TB and 2TB capacities, the SSD is priced at INR 25,649 and INR 50,349.
- It features an M.2 2280 form factor and includes AES 256-bit full-disk encryption.
- Endurance ratings are 400TBW for the 1TB model and 800TBW for the 2TB version.
- Sales are scheduled to begin in India during the final week of July 2026.
- The product includes a three-year limited warranty and support for Samsung Magician software.

### Why it Matters
This release provides a cost-effective storage alternative for budget-conscious users and secondary data setups amid ongoing global memory chip shortages. By prioritizing speed over long-term endurance, Samsung is targeting the entry-level market while maintaining its competitive presence in the high-performance hardware sector.
